result:
I keep thinking about the hinge of light that swings open every morning and how we walk through it without noticing. A door made of gold, set between two ordinary walls of time, and we step across the threshold still half-dreaming, coffee steam ghosting our faces, phones already warm in our palms. The miracle is not that the sun rises; the miracle is that we call it ordinary. I want to call it something else—Tuesday’s cathedral, maybe. A sky-roofed nave where sparrows hymn in place of choirs.

There is a quiet war inside the word enough. I wage it while slicing onions, while folding someone else’s socks, while staring at the spreadsheet that insists I quantify human attention in fourteen-minute increments. Somewhere inside me a child keeps asking, “But what are we counting toward?” and the adult I am supposed to be answers, “Hush, we need this job.” The child hides beneath the ribcage and paints galaxies on the underside of my lungs with a stolen biro. Every sigh I release is a balloon letter addressed to the night sky: “We are still alive down here, in case you were wondering.”

I met a woman last week who carries a tiny hourglass in her pocket filled, not with sand, but with the cremains of her dog. She turns it over when she feels the world tipping too fast toward meaninglessness. Time reverses for sixty dictated seconds; the ash refuses to fall. Instead, it climbs, grain by grain, reassembling into the shape of a tail that wags once, twice, before collapsing again into dust. She told me this story at a bus stop, as if confessing she sometimes shoplifts oranges. I wanted to say: “That is the most beautiful thing I’ve heard all year,” but the bus arrived panting, and politeness told me to board. I still don’t know whether stories like that are meant to be answered or simply witnessed.

Memory works like a loom run by drunken spiders. It threads the scent of cinnamon with my grandfather’s funeral, knots the sound of a typewriter into the first night I kissed someone who did not love me back. We end up wearing tapestries we would never purchase at market. I find my father’s anger tucked inside the pocket of every autumn; I find my mother’s lullabies braided into the shower curtain. The past is never past; it is upholstery.

I want to write a dictionary that defines only words we misuse. Lonely: the state of having no one to tell the strange dream to. Home: the place where your childhood fear of the dark is still faithfully kept for you, like a coat you forgot. Love: a verb masquerading as a noun, same as fire, same as river. I would mail copies to every government so laws would have to be rewritten: “You cannot imprison fire, cannot dam river, cannot bottle verb.” Imagine the headlines: Judges weeping over linguistics. Jails repurposed into libraries. The stock market collapses under the weight of unspoken feelings; we pay rent in sonnets instead.

Sometimes I suspect we are each an extra in someone else’s recurring dream. The barista who draws hearts on your latte might be the only proof another sleeper has that kindness exists. The stranger on the subway wearing mismatched shoes could be your own dream trying to signal, “Wake up, you left the stove on.” We are so busy rehearsing our starring roles we forget the entire production is improvised. The director is asleep in the aisle, script crumpled under seat 14B, and the ushers have unionized into constellations.

I read that the universe expands at 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec, which sounds like a math problem no one expects you to solve on the spot, but it means everything is running away from everything else like kids scattering when the streetlights blink on. Even the space between the atoms of my eyelash is stretching, so when I wink at you, that tiny farewell is cosmically ordained to become, eventually, a canyon. This should feel tragic, but instead it feels like permission: if separation is the law, then meeting is the miracle. We should greet each other like planets: “Wow, you too? In all this darkness?”

I keep a list of things I expect to outlive: my favorite pen, the neighbor’s tulips, most of my passwords, certainly the bee that just threw itself against the window three times. Yet the list of things I expect will outlive me is shorter and stranger: the smell after rain, the rumor that whales sing in rhyme, the fear that I’m not doing enough with the short time I have. I imagine death like standing at the lip of a canyon at night; someone on the opposite rim strikes a match, and for one flash I see every face I have ever loved arranged in a semicircle, waving. Then dark again. But the image of their hands remains, pressed like leaves into the book of the night.

Once, while lost in a foreign city, I asked an elderly man for directions. He replied by reciting a poem about geese. When I apologized for not understanding, he smiled and said, “Navigation is just another word for paying attention.” Then he walked me to the fountain I was looking for, which turned out to be dry, filled instead with rose petals someone had torn from bridal bouquets. We stood there in the hush of unpaid admission, two strangers sharing an empty stage, and I felt suddenly placed, like a letter finally slid into the correct slot. I never learned his name; I think of him whenever I arrive anywhere early enough to hear pigeons gossip.

We speak often of falling in love, rarely of rising in it. Yet every morning couples wake entangled, negotiate the architecture of each other’s breath, build small bridges from the island of one dream to another. That is labor. That is masonry. That is why the heart has four chambers: one for cartography, one for cartography’s mistakes, one for echo, one for encore. I have seen people open their chests like suitcases, offering the wrinkled shirt of their grief, expecting you to iron it with your bare hands. Somehow we do. Somehow the wrinkles release, steam rising like incense to whatever god watches over the domestic.

The last time I visited my grandmother, she mistook me for a letter I had written her in third grade. She read me out loud to herself, laughing at my misspelling of spaghetti, folding the memory into a paper crane she placed on the bedside table. When I left, she pressed into my palm not a tissue, not a candy, but the word “remember,” spoken as if it were a verb that tasted of metal. I carry it in my mouth like a coin I refuse to spend; every time I speak, it clinks against my teeth, reminding me currencies other than minutes exist.

I want to tell you that it is enough, all of it. The spilled coffee, the unread messages, the calendar squares bloated with obligations, the night you stood in the supermarket aisle unable to choose between brands of soap because suddenly the difference felt cosmic. I want to say: the hinge of light is still there, even when offices replace the sky with ceiling panels, even when your own heartbeat sounds like someone else’s knock at an inconvenient hour. Step through. Step through. The day is a cathedral; the day is a bus stop; the day is a dog reassembling from ash. Whatever it is, it only asks that you keep walking forward carrying someone else’s strange dream in your pocket like a tiny hourglass, turning it over whenever the world tilts, which is always, which is now, which is forever.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
